FITNESS · PLAYBOOK · MAY 2026

How to open a gym in 2026.

The 11-step playbook for first-time gym owners. From market research and format selection through financing, buildout, the AI-native tech stack, presale campaign, and grand opening. Built from operator interviews and Treetop's observation across multi-unit fitness engagements.

The honest framing

Before you start

Most new gyms fail not because the owner had a bad idea, but because they ran out of cash in months 4-9 before membership reached breakeven. The single best thing a first-time owner can do is take the time to build a realistic working-capital plan before signing a lease — not after.

This playbook is sequenced to surface that reality early: research and financial model come before lease, lease comes before buildout, presale runs in parallel with buildout. Skip steps at your own risk.

Typical timeline: 9-15 months for first-time owners. Personal training studios can compress to 4-7 months; full-service gyms often need 12-18 months. See the full timeline breakdown.

Step 1

01Market research and format selection

Before anything else: pick the gym format that matches your market's actual demand and your available capital. The right format for a Tier-3 suburban market is rarely the right format for a Tier-1 urban core.

  • Drive-time analysis: Map every competing gym within a 12-minute drive. Note format, price point, hours, member count if knowable.
  • Demographic fit: Median household income, age skew, density. CrossFit affiliates work in walkable urban + dense suburban; full-service gyms need population density to support headcount.
  • Format choices: Personal training studio · Small boutique · Mid-size group fitness · CrossFit affiliate · Full-service · Boutique franchise. See the cost guide for cost ranges by format.
  • Honest question to answer: "Why would a member leave their current gym to come to mine?" If you can't answer in one sentence, the market research is incomplete.
Step 2

02Business plan and financial model

Lenders require one. The act of building one forces you to confront the math.

  • 36-month financial model: Month-by-month revenue ramp, fixed and variable costs, breakeven membership count, cash position by month.
  • Unit economics: Revenue per member, fully-loaded cost to acquire a member, member lifetime, contribution margin per member-month.
  • Working capital plan: 6-12 months of operating expenses funded separately from startup costs. This is the most underbudgeted line item.
  • Sensitivity scenarios: What happens if presale converts at 60% of plan? At 40%? At 25%? Lenders will ask.

Template: /gym-business-plan.

Step 3

03Secure financing

Almost no first-time owner self-funds an entire build. Plan for a stacked capital structure:

  • SBA 7(a) loan: Up to $5M, 10-25 year terms, ~10-30% down. 60-120 days to close. Most-common path for first-time gym owners.
  • Equipment leasing: 36-60 month terms, low/no money down on equipment. Often layered on top of SBA.
  • Landlord TI (tenant improvement) allowance: $20-60/sq ft typical for second-generation retail. Negotiable. Effectively free buildout capital.
  • Seller financing: If buying an existing gym, 10-30% of price commonly carried by seller at 6-9% interest.
  • Friends and family / ROBS / personal capital: The required equity stake.

Full breakdown: /how-to-finance-a-gym.

Step 4

04Form the legal entity

  • LLC vs S-Corp: Most single-location gyms form as LLCs taxed as S-Corps. Ask your CPA.
  • EIN, state registration, business bank account, business credit card.
  • Insurance binders: General liability, professional liability, workers comp, property. Required before lease execution.
  • Music licensing accounts: ASCAP + BMI + SESAC. ~$1K-$4K/year per a typical gym.
Step 5

05Site selection and lease negotiation

Location is the single biggest determinant of long-term success. Spend the time.

  • Drive-time/walk-time: 80% of members live or work within 12 minutes of the gym. Visibility from a major road helps but isn't required.
  • Anchor co-tenants: Grocery, Target, Whole Foods, coffee, daycare. These pull recurring foot traffic past your door.
  • Parking ratio: 1 space per 200 sq ft minimum for most formats; more for full-service gyms with peak-hour crowding.
  • Lease terms: 5+5 or 10-year primary; TI allowance; rent abatement during buildout (4-6 months typical); CAM caps; personal guarantee scope (cap it if possible).
  • Demographic fit: Use Esri Tapestry or SimplyAnalytics on the trade area before signing.

The trap: Cheap rent in the wrong location is the most-expensive lease you can sign. A 30% rent premium for the right co-tenants and demographic mix usually pays back 3-5x in member acquisition cost.

Step 6

06Permits and buildout

  • Building permit: 30-90 day approval window typical. Slow markets (SF, NYC, Boston) can run 4-6 months.
  • Contractor selection: Get three bids. Reference-check the last two gym projects each has done. Fixed-price contract with milestone draws.
  • Construction window: 60-150 days for most second-generation retail. Ground-up construction is 9-18 months and rarely worth it for a first gym.
  • Critical-path items: HVAC sizing (sweat + odor + heat from cardio is real), flooring (rubber spec matters), electrical for cardio + sound, locker room plumbing, ADA compliance.
Step 7

07Equipment procurement and install

  • Buy vs lease decision: Lease cardio (depreciates fast, breaks); buy strength + functional (holds value, rarely breaks).
  • Used vs new: Auctions and gym closures can yield 40-60% off retail on commercial-grade strength equipment.
  • Vendor diversification: Don't tie 100% of equipment to a single vendor — service responsiveness varies, and lock-in pricing hurts later.
  • Install scheduling: Equipment arrives 6-12 weeks after order. Schedule install to land 7-10 days before soft open.

Decision framework: /should-i-buy-or-lease-gym-equipment.

Step 8

08Tech stack: CRM and AI agents

This is the category where 2026 is meaningfully different. The legacy gym software stack used to require $8K-$20K/year in software fees plus 1-2 admin staff. AI agent platforms collapse much of that into a single subscription.

Minimum viable tech stack (2026)

  • Gym CRM as system of record: MindBody, Glofox, Pike13, Zen Planner. $2K-$5K/year.
  • AI agent platform: Handles lead nurture, no-show recovery, member retention, member coaching. $3K-$12K/year for a small gym. Purpose-built leader: fitagentic.ai.
  • Payments: Stripe or Square (often integrated with CRM).
  • Member-facing app: Usually included with CRM or AI platform.

The hidden savings: AI agents handling lead nurture + no-show recovery + member-facing coaching often eliminate 1-2 front-desk/membership-consultant hires entirely. That's $40K-$80K per avoided hire in year one — usually the difference between surviving and not.

Step 9

09Hire and certify staff

  • Lean opening team: Owner-operator + 1-2 trainers + 1-2 coaches. Hire the GM later if needed.
  • Role design: Avoid the "front desk + everything else" job description. AI agents now handle most of "everything else."
  • Certifications: NASM, NSCA, ACE for personal training. CrossFit Level 1 for affiliates. Bookend with CPR/AED requirement.
  • Payroll setup: Gusto or Justworks for a small team; ADP if scaling fast.
Step 10

10Run the 90-day presale

The single highest-leverage activity in the entire opening sequence. Presale memberships pay for the working-capital months that follow. Skip or underinvest in presale and you'll spend the next 12 months catching up.

  • Founding-member offer: Locked-in lifetime rate at 60-70% of grand-open price. Cap the cohort.
  • Channel mix: Local Meta ads, Google Local, walk-by signage at the buildout site, partnerships with adjacent businesses, referral program.
  • Conversion benchmark: 30-50% of qualified leads should convert during presale. Below 25% means offer or pricing is off.
  • AI agent role: Lead nurture sequences, no-show recovery for tours, presale-member onboarding before the doors open.

Full playbook: /gym-pre-sale-playbook.

Step 11

11Soft open, then grand open

  • Soft open: 7-14 days with founding members only. Stress-test class scheduling, check-in flow, payment processing, locker rooms, cleaning cadence. Fix everything.
  • Grand open: Public launch with full marketing push, opening event, press coverage if relevant.
  • First 90 days post-open: Daily standups on member onboarding, churn signals, NPS. The first 30 days of a new member's experience predict their 12-month retention more than anything else that follows.

The post-open mistake: Most owners treat grand open as the finish line. It's not — it's the starting line of the working-capital survival window. Months 4-9 post-open are where most failures happen.
